children.value: Trigonometry as geometry on the unit circle.
Sine and cosine are the coordinates of a point rotating around a circle of radius one.
The Pythagorean identity, angle-sum formulas, and power-reduction formulas that recur throughout quantum mechanics.
A complex number carries a real and an imaginary part and lives in a plane.

children.html: eiϕ=cos⁡ϕ+isin⁡ϕe^{i\phi} = \cos\phi + i\sin\phieiϕ=cosϕ+isinϕ

children.value: fuses trigonometry and exponentials into one object and is the single most useful identity in the course.
Multiplying complex numbers rotates and scales them, and the conjugate reflects across the real axis.

Trigonometry and complex numbers are two views of the same thing: rotation in a plane. Waves, oscillations, and quantum phases all live here, and Euler’s formula is the bridge that connects them. We treat them together because in quantum mechanics you almost never use one without the other.
Sine and cosine on the unit circle

children.label: Sine and cosine on the unit circle

children.value: Take a point on a circle of radius
and rotate it counterclockwise by an angle

children.html: θ\thetaθ
2π2\pi2π
x=cos⁡θ,y=sin⁡θ.x = \cos\theta, \qquad y = \sin\theta.x=cosθ,y=sinθ.

children.value: Cosine is the horizontal coordinate, sine the vertical one, and the tangent

children.html: tan⁡θ=sin⁡θ/cos⁡θ\tan\theta = \sin\theta/\cos\thetatanθ=sinθ/cosθ

children.value: is the slope of the radius.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

theta = np.deg2rad(50)
t = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 400)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.5, 5.5))
ax.plot(np.cos(t), np.sin(t), 'k', lw=1.5)           # unit circle
ax.plot([0, np.cos(theta)], [0, np.sin(theta)], color='#2e4057', lw=2)  # radius
ax.plot([np.cos(theta), np.cos(theta)], [0, np.sin(theta)], color='#66a182', lw=2, label=r'$\sin\theta$')
ax.plot([0, np.cos(theta)], [0, 0], color='#d1495b', lw=2, label=r'$\cos\theta$')
ax.plot(np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta), 'ko', ms=6)

ax.annotate(r'$(\cos\theta,\ \sin\theta)$', (np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)),
            textcoords='offset points', xytext=(8, 8), fontsize=11)
ax.axhline(0, color='gray', lw=0.6)
ax.axvline(0, color='gray', lw=0.6)
ax.set_aspect('equal')
ax.set_xlim(-1.3, 1.5)
ax.set_ylim(-1.3, 1.3)
ax.legend(loc='lower left', fontsize=10)
ax.set_title(r'Fig.1 Sine and cosine as coordinates on the unit circle ($\theta=50^\circ$)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

children.html: θ\thetaθ

children.value: runs around the circle, the two coordinates trace out the familiar waves: cosine starts at
, and each is the other shifted by a quarter turn.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 400)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.2))
ax.plot(t, np.cos(t), color='#d1495b', lw=2, label=r'$\cos\theta$')
ax.plot(t, np.sin(t), color='#66a182', lw=2, label=r'$\sin\theta$')
ax.axhline(0, color='gray', lw=0.6)
ax.set_xticks([0, np.pi / 2, np.pi, 3 * np.pi / 2, 2 * np.pi])
ax.set_xticklabels(['0', r'$\pi/2$', r'$\pi$', r'$3\pi/2$', r'$2\pi$'])
ax.set_xlabel(r'$\theta$ (radians)')
ax.legend(fontsize=10, loc='upper right')
ax.set_title('Fig.2 Cosine and sine as functions of the angle, offset by a quarter turn')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Because the point lies on a unit circle, its coordinates always satisfy the

\sin(A \pm B) = \sin A\cos B \pm \cos A\sin B, \qquad
\cos(A \pm B) = \cos A\cos B \mp \sin A\sin B,

children.value: , which are what let us linearize a pendulum or a vibrating bond near equilibrium.
Rather than memorize the whole table of identities, it is easier to derive them from Euler’s formula, which we build next.

children.value: is a two-dimensional number: it needs two components for its full specification. They arise the moment we ask for the roots of a simple polynomial. The equation

children.html: x2+1=0⟹x1,2=±−1=±ix^2 + 1 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x_{1,2} = \pm\sqrt{-1} = \pm ix2+1=0⟹x1,2​=±−1​=±i

children.value: has no real solution, so we extend the real line

children.value: The plot below shows why polar form is natural: the same complex number is one dot in the plane, described equally well by

children.html: (x,y)(x, y)(x,y)
(r,ϕ)(r, \phi)(r,ϕ)

children.value:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

z = 2 + 1.5j
r, phi = np.abs(z), np.angle(z)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.5, 5))
ax.annotate('', xy=(z.real, z.imag), xytext=(0, 0),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='#2e4057', lw=2))
arc = np.linspace(0, phi, 60)
ax.plot(0.6 * np.cos(arc), 0.6 * np.sin(arc), color='#d1495b', lw=1.5)
ax.plot([0, z.real], [z.imag, z.imag], '--', color='gray', lw=1)
ax.plot([z.real, z.real], [0, z.imag], '--', color='gray', lw=1)

ax.text(z.real + 0.1, z.imag + 0.1, fr'$z = {z.real:.0f}+{z.imag:.1f}i = {r:.2f}\,e^{{i\phi}}$', fontsize=11)
ax.text(0.7, 0.15, r'$\phi$', color='#d1495b', fontsize=13)
ax.text(z.real / 2, -0.25, r'$x=r\cos\phi$', color='gray', fontsize=9, ha='center')
ax.text(z.real + 0.05, z.imag / 2, r'$y=r\sin\phi$', color='gray', fontsize=9)

ax.axhline(0, color='black', lw=0.8)
ax.axvline(0, color='black', lw=0.8)
ax.set_xlim(-0.6, 3)
ax.set_ylim(-0.6, 2.4)
ax.set_aspect('equal')
ax.set_xlabel('Real')
ax.set_ylabel('Imaginary')
ax.set_title('Fig.4 The same number in Cartesian (x, y) and polar (r, phi) form')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

children.value: Multiplying a number by its conjugate rotates it back onto the real axis and returns a non-negative real number, the squared magnitude:

children.html: ∣z∣2=zˉ z=(x−iy)(x+iy)=x2+y2=r2.|z|^2 = \bar{z}\,z = (x - iy)(x + iy) = x^2 + y^2 = r^2.∣z∣2=zˉz=(x−iy)(x+iy)=x2+y2=r2.

children.value: This is exactly the operation that turns a quantum amplitude

children.html: ψ\psiψ
∣ψ∣2=ψ∗ψ|\psi|^2 = \psi^*\psi∣ψ∣2=ψ∗ψ

zˉ\bar{z}zˉ

children.value: recovers the trig functions as exponentials, a substitution that simplifies countless integrals:

children.html: cos⁡ϕ=eiϕ+e−iϕ2,sin⁡ϕ=eiϕ−e−iϕ2i.\cos\phi = \frac{e^{i\phi} + e^{-i\phi}}{2}, \qquad \sin\phi = \frac{e^{i\phi} - e^{-i\phi}}{2i}.cosϕ=2eiϕ+e−iϕ​,sinϕ=2ieiϕ−e−iϕ​.

children.value: Deriving the angle-sum identities from Euler’s formula

children.html: ei(ω+θ)=eiωeiθe^{i(\omega+\theta)} = e^{i\omega}e^{i\theta}ei(ω+θ)=eiωeiθ

children.value: and match real and imaginary parts:

children.html: cos⁡(ω+θ)+isin⁡(ω+θ)=(cos⁡ω+isin⁡ω)(cos⁡θ+isin⁡θ).\cos(\omega+\theta) + i\sin(\omega+\theta) = (\cos\omega + i\sin\omega)(\cos\theta + i\sin\theta).cos(ω+θ)+isin(ω+θ)=(cosω+isinω)(cosθ+isinθ).

children.value: Multiplying out the right side and equating parts gives both angle-sum identities at once:

children.html: cos⁡(ω+θ)=cos⁡ωcos⁡θ−sin⁡ωsin⁡θ,sin⁡(ω+θ)=sin⁡ωcos⁡θ+cos⁡ωsin⁡θ.\cos(\omega+\theta) = \cos\omega\cos\theta - \sin\omega\sin\theta, \qquad \sin(\omega+\theta) = \sin\omega\cos\theta + \cos\omega\sin\theta.cos(ω+θ)=cosωcosθ−sinωsinθ,sin(ω+θ)=sinωcosθ+cosωsinθ.

children.value: This is why we do not memorize the trig identities separately: they all fall out of one exponential rule.

children.html: (cos⁡θ+isin⁡θ)n=cos⁡nθ+isin⁡nθ(\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)^n = \cos n\theta + i\sin n\theta(cosθ+isinθ)n=cosnθ+isinnθ
(eiθ)n=einθ(e^{i\theta})^n = e^{in\theta}(eiθ)n=einθ

children.value: Where this shows up in chemistry
Complex exponentials are the natural language of anything that oscillates or diffracts. In X-ray crystallography, each scattered wave is a complex number

children.html: A=∣A∣eiϕA = |A|e^{i\phi}A=∣A∣eiϕ
F(h)=∑jfj eih⋅rjF(\mathbf{h}) = \sum_j f_j\,e^{i\mathbf{h}\cdot\mathbf{r}_j}F(h)=∑j​fj​eih⋅rj​

children.value: combines these amplitudes and phases to reconstruct electron density by an inverse Fourier transform. The hard part, the
